what: Calculate Individual-Level Predicted Coefficients from beezdemand_nlme Model — get_individual_coefficients
title: Calculate Individual-Level Predicted Coefficients from beezdemand_nlme Model — get_individual_coefficients
text: function extracts combines fixed random effects calculate individual-level predicted coefficients parameter-factor combinations beezdemand_nlme model object. automatically detects factor structure calculates coefficients individual factor level.

title: Calculate Individual-Level Predicted Coefficients from beezdemand_nlme Model — get_individual_coefficients
code: get_individual_coefficients(   fit_obj,   params = c("Q0", "alpha"),   format = c("wide", "long") )

title: Calculate Individual-Level Predicted Coefficients from beezdemand_nlme Model — get_individual_coefficients
text: fit_obj beezdemand_nlme object returned fit_demand_mixed(). params Character vector specifying parameters calculate. Options "Q0", "alpha", c("Q0", "alpha"). Default c("Q0", "alpha"). format Character, output format. "wide" returns one row per individual separate columns parameter-factor combination. "long" returns one row per individual-parameter-factor combination. Default "wide".

title: Calculate Individual-Level Predicted Coefficients from beezdemand_nlme Model — get_individual_coefficients
text: data frame individual-level predicted coefficients. "wide" format: rows individuals, columns parameter-factor combinations "long" format: columns id, parameter, condition, coefficient_value Column naming convention wide format: estimated_\{param\}_intercept: Baseline/reference level coefficient estimated_\{param\}_\{factor\}\{level\}: Factor level-specific coefficient coefficients log10 scale (model estimation scale). convert natural scale, use 10^coefficient.

title: Calculate Individual-Level Predicted Coefficients from beezdemand_nlme Model — get_individual_coefficients
text: Individual-level coefficients represent predicted parameter values subject study. models factors, coefficients combine: baseline intercept effect (fixed + random) factor-specific effect (fixed + random) factor level equivalent manually calculating: coefficient = intercept_fixed + intercept_random + factor_fixed + factor_random function automatically handles: Models without factors number factor levels Missing random effects (defaults 0) Complex factor structures multiple factors models without factors, intercept coefficients calculated. models factors, intercept factor-level coefficients provided.

title: Calculate Individual-Level Predicted Coefficients from beezdemand_nlme Model — get_individual_coefficients
code: # \donttest{ data(ko) fit <- fit_demand_mixed(ko, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",                         id_var = "monkey", factors = "drug",                         equation_form = "zben") #> Generating starting values using method: 'heuristic' #> Using heuristic method for starting values. #> --- Fitting NLME Model --- #> Equation Form: zben #> Param Space: log10 #> NLME Formula: y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> Start values (first few): Q0_int=2.27, alpha_int=-3 #> Number of fixed parameters: 6 (Q0: 3, alpha: 3) individual_coefs <- get_individual_coefficients(fit) head(individual_coefs) #>   id estimated_Q0_intercept estimated_Q0_drugFentanyl #> 1  A               2.133994                  1.969328 #> 2  B               2.133994                  1.969328 #> 3  C               2.133994                  1.969328 #>   estimated_Q0_drugRemifentanil estimated_alpha_intercept #> 1                      2.384881                 -4.663837 #> 2                      2.384881                 -4.663837 #> 3                      2.384881                 -4.663837 #>   estimated_alpha_drugFentanyl estimated_alpha_drugRemifentanil #> 1                    -4.393874                        -4.708936 #> 2                    -4.393874                        -4.708936 #> 3                    -4.393874                        -4.708936 # }

what: Calculate K Scaling Parameter for Demand Curve Fitting — get_k
title: Calculate K Scaling Parameter for Demand Curve Fitting — get_k
text: Calculates k scaling parameter used demand curve equations normalize consumption across different units ranges. k value derived logarithmic range consumption values. modern replacement GetK(), explicit parameters adjustment value optional verbose output.

title: Calculate K Scaling Parameter for Demand Curve Fitting — get_k
code: get_k(   data,   use_means = TRUE,   adjustment = 0.5,   x_var = "x",   y_var = "y",   verbose = FALSE )

title: Calculate K Scaling Parameter for Demand Curve Fitting — get_k
text: data data frame long format columns price consumption use_means Logical indicating whether calculate k mean consumption price (TRUE, default) individual consumption values (FALSE) adjustment Numeric adjustment added log range (default: 0.5). value ensures k slightly larger observed range. x_var Character string specifying column name price (default: "x") y_var Character string specifying column name consumption (default: "y") verbose Logical indicating whether print calculation details (default: FALSE)

title: Calculate K Scaling Parameter for Demand Curve Fitting — get_k
text: single numeric value representing k scaling parameter

title: Calculate K Scaling Parameter for Demand Curve Fitting — get_k
text: k parameter calculated : $$k = \log_{10}(\text{max}) - \log_{10}(\text{min}) + \text{adjustment}$$ max min maximum minimum non-zero consumption values.

title: Calculate K Scaling Parameter for Demand Curve Fitting — get_k
text: k parameter appears several demand curve equations: Hursh & Silberberg (2008): Scales exponential term Koffarnus et al. (2015): Normalizes exponentiated model Ensures numerical stability model fitting

title: Calculate K Scaling Parameter for Demand Curve Fitting — get_k
text: use_means = TRUE (default): Calculates k mean consumption price point. Recommended data multiple subjects, reduces influence individual outliers. use_means = FALSE: Calculates k full range individual consumption values. May preferable single-subject data individual variability theoretically important.

title: Calculate K Scaling Parameter for Demand Curve Fitting — get_k
text: non-zero consumption values used (zero values excluded) Missing values (NA) automatically removed via na.rm = TRUE default adjustment 0.5 conventional can modified

title: Calculate K Scaling Parameter for Demand Curve Fitting — get_k
code: # \donttest{ data(apt, package = "beezdemand")  # Calculate k using default settings (mean range + 0.5) k_val <- get_k(apt)  # Calculate k from individual values k_ind <- get_k(apt, use_means = FALSE)  # Calculate with custom adjustment k_custom <- get_k(apt, adjustment = 1.0)  # Show calculation details k_verbose <- get_k(apt, verbose = TRUE) #> Calculating k from mean consumption values: #>   Max mean: 6.80 #>   Min mean: 0.80 #>   log10(6.80) - log10(0.80) + 0.50 = 1.429 # }

text: function wrapper around get_demand_param_emms. first calls get_demand_param_emms calculate Estimated Marginal Means (EMMs) Q0 alpha parameters combinations specified factor levels. filters results return EMMs combinations factor levels actually present original dataset used fit beezdemand_nlme model.

title: Get Estimated Marginal Means for Observed Factor Combinations — get_observed_demand_param_emms
text: fit_obj beezdemand_nlme object returned fit_demand_mixed(). factors_in_emm Character vector factor names compute EMMs . Defaults factors present fit_obj. factors define grid EMMs initially calculated filtered. Optional named list specifying levels conditioning variables emmeans::ref_grid(). Passed get_demand_param_emms. ci_level Confidence level EMMs (default 0.95). Passed get_demand_param_emms. include_ev Logical. TRUE, calculates includes Essential Value (EV) derived alpha. Passed get_demand_param_emms. Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments passed get_demand_param_emms subsequently emmeans::emmeans().

title: Get Estimated Marginal Means for Observed Factor Combinations — get_observed_demand_param_emms
text: tibble similar output get_demand_param_emms, filtered include rows corresponding factor level combinations observed original fit_obj$data. Contains: Factor levels Columns factor factors_in_emm. Q0_param_log10, alpha_param_log10 EMMs model parameters (log10 scale) CIs. Q0_natural, alpha_natural EMMs back-transformed natural scale CIs. EV, LCL_EV, UCL_EV (include_ev=TRUE) Essential Value CI.

title: Get Estimated Marginal Means for Observed Factor Combinations — get_observed_demand_param_emms
code: # \donttest{ data(ko, package = "beezdemand") ko$y_ll4 <- ll4(ko$y, lambda = 4) fit <- fit_demand_mixed(ko, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",   id_var = "monkey", factors = "dose", equation_form = "zben") #> Generating starting values using method: 'heuristic' #> Using heuristic method for starting values. #> --- Fitting NLME Model --- #> Equation Form: zben #> Param Space: log10 #> NLME Formula: y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> Start values (first few): Q0_int=2.27, alpha_int=-3 #> Number of fixed parameters: 10 (Q0: 5, alpha: 5) get_observed_demand_param_emms(fit) #> # A tibble: 5 × 13 #>   dose  Q0_param_log10 LCL_Q0_param_log10 UCL_Q0_param_log10 Q0_natural #>   <fct>          <dbl>              <dbl>              <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 3e-05           2.58               2.35               2.80      377.  #> 2 1e-04           2.38               2.23               2.52      238.  #> 3 3e-04           2.21               2.10               2.32      163.  #> 4 0.001           1.91               1.78               2.03       80.5 #> 5 0.003           1.90               1.73               2.07       79.7 #> # ℹ 8 more variables: LCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, UCL_Q0_natural <dbl>, #> #   alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, LCL_alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, #> #   UCL_alpha_param_log10 <dbl>, alpha_natural <dbl>, LCL_alpha_natural <dbl>, #> #   UCL_alpha_natural <dbl> # }

title: Log-Logistic Transformation (LL4-like) — ll4
text: Applies log-logistic like transformation, specifically log_base(x^lambda + 1) / lambda. transformation useful compressing data spans several orders magnitude handling zero values gracefully (x=0 yields 0). variation related Box-Cox transformation generalized logarithm.

title: Log-Logistic Transformation (LL4-like) — ll4
code: ll4(x, lambda = 4, base = 10)

title: Log-Logistic Transformation (LL4-like) — ll4
text: x numeric vector scalar non-negative values transformed. lambda positive numeric scalar, lambda parameter transformation. Controls curvature. Default 4. base positive numeric scalar, base logarithm. Default 10.

title: Log-Logistic Transformation (LL4-like) — ll4
text: numeric vector scalar transformed values. Returns NaN x < 0 lambda results non-real numbers (e.g., even root negative). However, intended domain x >= 0.

title: Log-Logistic Transformation (LL4-like) — ll4
Inverse Log-Logistic Transformation (Inverse LL4-like) — ll4_inv
text: Applies inverse ll4 transformation. Given y = ll4(x), function calculates x = (base^(y * lambda) - 1)^(1/lambda).

title: Inverse Log-Logistic Transformation (Inverse LL4-like) — ll4_inv
code: ll4_inv(y, lambda = 4, base = 10)

title: Inverse Log-Logistic Transformation (Inverse LL4-like) — ll4_inv
text: y numeric vector scalar transformed values (output ll4). lambda positive numeric scalar, lambda parameter used original ll4 transformation. Must match one used forward transform. Default 4. base positive numeric scalar, base logarithm used original ll4 transformation. Must match. Default 10.

title: Inverse Log-Logistic Transformation (Inverse LL4-like) — ll4_inv
text: numeric vector scalar original, untransformed values. May return NaN (base^(y * lambda) - 1) negative 1/lambda implies even root (e.g., lambda 2 4).

title: Inverse Log-Logistic Transformation (Inverse LL4-like) — ll4_inv
code: original_values <- c(0, 1, 10, 100, 1000) transformed_values <- ll4(original_values) back_transformed_values <- ll4_inv(transformed_values) print(data.frame(original_values, transformed_values, back_transformed_values)) #>   original_values transformed_values back_transformed_values #> 1               0          0.0000000                       0 #> 2               1          0.0752575                       1 #> 3              10          1.0000109                      10 #> 4             100          2.0000000                     100 #> 5            1000          3.0000000                    1000 all.equal(original_values, back_transformed_values) # Should be TRUE or very close #> [1] TRUE  # Example with negative y (log-transformed value) # If y_ll4 = -0.5 (meaning original value was between 0 and 1 for log10) ll4_inv(-0.5, lambda = 4, base = 10) # (10^(-0.5*4) - 1)^(1/4) = (0.01 - 1)^(1/4) -> NaN #> [1] NaN # The ll4_inv function as provided will return NaN here. # A more robust version for demand might floor at 0 if NaN occurs.

what: Parameter Naming Registry for beezdemand — param-registry
title: Parameter Naming Registry for beezdemand — param-registry
text: file defines canonical parameter naming conventions across model families. serves single source truth parameter names, scales, mappings.

title: Parameter Naming Registry for beezdemand — param-registry
text: Parameters follow snake_case scale prefixes: log10_<param>: parameter log10 space (e.g., log10_alpha) log_<param>: parameter natural log space (e.g., log_alpha) natural_<param>: parameter natural/linear space (e.g., natural_alpha) <param>: canonical name without prefix (context determines scale)

title: Parameter Naming Registry for beezdemand — param-registry
text: Equations grouped model family: NLS (FitCurves): hs, koff, linear. k parameter defaults fixed supports multiple modes: "fit", "ind", "share", "range". NLME (fit_demand_mixed): zben, nlme_simplified, nlme_exponentiated. nlme_exponentiated uses k (always fixed). Hurdle Part II (fit_demand_hurdle): hurdle_zhao, hurdle_hs, hurdle_snd. Parameters log space. hurdle_snd k parameter. variant 2RE 3RE sub-models different variance/correlation parameters. Cross-price (fit_cp_nls): cp_exponential, cp_exponentiated, cp_additive. use log10_qalone, , log10_beta.

title: Parameter Naming Registry for beezdemand — param-registry
text: pmax_model: Pmax derived fitted model parameters pmax_obs: Pmax observed/empirical data (max expenditure price) omax_model: Omax derived fitted model parameters omax_obs: Omax observed/empirical data alpha_star: Normalized alpha (Strategy B; Rzeszutek et al., 2025). Makes alpha comparable across different k values adjusting scaling constant. Computed \(\alpha^* = -\alpha / \ln(1 - 1/(k \cdot     \ln(b)))\) \(b\) logarithmic base (10 HS/Koff, \(e\) hurdle models). Returned FitCurves, tidy() fixed fits, tidy() hurdle fits. Standard error (alpha_star_se) obtained via delta method. Requires \(k \cdot \ln(b) > 1\); otherwise NA returned.

title: Parameter Naming Registry for beezdemand — param-registry
text: Legacy FitCurves output uses different naming. mappings : Pmaxd: derived Pmax model (approximate formula) Pmaxa: analytic Pmax Lambert W Pmaxe: empirical/observed Pmax data Omaxd: derived Omax model Omaxa: analytic Omax Lambert W Omaxe: empirical/observed Omax data Q0d: fitted Q0 (intensity) Alpha: fitted alpha (elasticity parameter) K: k parameter (range log units)

what: Reshape Demand Data Between Wide and Long Formats — pivot_demand_data
title: Reshape Demand Data Between Wide and Long Formats — pivot_demand_data
text: Converts demand data wide format (one row per subject, prices columns) long format (one row per observation id, x, y columns). convenience wrapper around tidyr::pivot_longer() tidyr::pivot_wider() tailored behavioral economic purchase task data.

title: Reshape Demand Data Between Wide and Long Formats — pivot_demand_data
code: pivot_demand_data(   data,   format = c("long", "wide"),   id_var = "id",   x_var = "x",   y_var = "y",   price_cols = NULL,   x_values = NULL,   drop_na = TRUE )

title: Reshape Demand Data Between Wide and Long Formats — pivot_demand_data
text: data data frame tibble reshape. format Character. Direction reshaping: "long" (wide long) "wide" (long wide). Default "long". id_var Character. Name subject/series identifier column. Default "id". x_var Character. Name price column long-format data. Used format = "wide". Default "x". y_var Character. Name consumption column long-format data. Used format = "wide". Default "y". price_cols Character vector column names wide-format data represent prices. Used format = "long". Default NULL, auto-detects: columns whose names parse numeric become price columns, remaining columns (besides id_var) preserved identifiers. column names parse numeric, non-id_var columns treated price columns (x_values must supplied). x_values Numeric vector actual price values corresponding price column wide-format data. Used format = "long". Must length number price columns. Default NULL, parses prices column names. drop_na Logical. format = "long", drop rows consumption (y) NA pivoting? Default TRUE. warning issued rows dropped.

title: Reshape Demand Data Between Wide and Long Formats — pivot_demand_data
text: tibble. format = "long": columns id, extra identifier columns, x (numeric price), y (numeric consumption). format = "wide": one row per subject prices column names.

title: Reshape Demand Data Between Wide and Long Formats — pivot_demand_data
text: function determines columns price columns : price_cols provided, columns used directly. price_cols NULL, column names tested .numeric(). Columns whose names successfully parse numbers (e.g., "0", "0.5", "10") treated price columns. Remaining non-id_var columns preserved extra identifiers. column names parse numeric, non-id_var columns become price columns x_values must supplied. Actual numeric price values come x_values supplied, parsing column names. column names parsed x_values supplied, error raised.

title: Reshape Demand Data Between Wide and Long Formats — pivot_demand_data
text: Pivots long data unique value x_var becomes column, values y_var. columns except x_var y_var used identifiers.

title: Reshape Demand Data Between Wide and Long Formats — pivot_demand_data
code: # --- Wide to long --- # Columns named as prices (auto-parsed) wide_num <- data.frame(   id = 1:3,   "0" = c(10, 8, 12), "0.5" = c(9, 7, 11), "1" = c(8, 6, 9),   check.names = FALSE ) pivot_demand_data(wide_num, format = "long") #> # A tibble: 9 × 3 #>      id     x     y #>   <int> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1     1   0      10 #> 2     1   0.5     9 #> 3     1   1       8 #> 4     2   0       8 #> 5     2   0.5     7 #> 6     2   1       6 #> 7     3   0      12 #> 8     3   0.5    11 #> 9     3   1       9  # Columns with non-numeric names require x_values wide_named <- data.frame(id = 1:2, price_1 = c(10, 8), price_2 = c(5, 4)) pivot_demand_data(wide_named, format = "long",                   x_values = c(0, 0.5)) #> # A tibble: 4 × 3 #>      id     x     y #>   <int> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1     1   0      10 #> 2     1   0.5     5 #> 3     2   0       8 #> 4     2   0.5     4  # --- Long to wide --- data(apt, package = "beezdemand") wide_apt <- pivot_demand_data(apt, format = "wide") head(wide_apt) #> # A tibble: 6 × 17 #>      id   `0` `0.5`   `1` `1.5`   `2` `2.5`   `3`   `4`   `5`   `6`   `7`   `8` #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1    19    10    10    10     8     8     8     7     7     7     6     6     5 #> 2    30     3     3     3     3     2     2     2     2     2     2     2     2 #> 3    38     4     4     4     4     4     4     4     3     3     3     3     2 #> 4    60    10    10     8     8     6     6     5     5     4     4     3     3 #> 5    68    10    10     9     9     8     8     7     6     5     5     5     4 #> 6   106     5     5     5     5     4     4     4     3     3     2     2     0 #> # ℹ 4 more variables: `9` <dbl>, `10` <dbl>, `15` <dbl>, `20` <dbl>

what: Predict Method for beezdemand_nlme Objects — predict.beezdemand_nlme
title: Predict Method for beezdemand_nlme Objects — predict.beezdemand_nlme
text: Generates point predictions fitted beezdemand_nlme model. Predictions can made population level (fixed effects ) group/subject level (fixed + random effects). output scale depends equation_form used model fitting whether inv_fun applied.

title: Predict Method for beezdemand_nlme Objects — predict.beezdemand_nlme
code: # S3 method for class 'beezdemand_nlme' predict(   object,   newdata = NULL,   type = c("response", "link", "population", "individual"),   level = 0,   inv_fun = identity,   se.fit = FALSE,   interval = c("none", "confidence"),   interval_level = 0.95,   ... )

title: Predict Method for beezdemand_nlme Objects — predict.beezdemand_nlme
text: object beezdemand_nlme object. newdata Optional data frame make predictions. Must contain x_var factors specified original model. group-level predictions desired (level=1), id_var column original fit must also present newdata levels correspond original data meaningful random effect application. NULL, predictions made data used fitting model. type One "response" (default), "link", "population", "individual". "population" "individual" aliases set level 0 1, respectively. level Integer, prediction level nlme::predict.nlme(): 0: Population predictions (based fixed effects ). 1 (higher, number grouping levels model): Group-specific predictions (fixed effects + random effects specified id_var level). Default 0. inv_fun Optional function inverse-transform predictions. Example: y_var log10-transformed fitting equation_form like "zben" produces predictions log10 scale, inv_fun = function(x) 10^x convert predictions back original consumption scale. equation_form "simplified" (models raw Y), inv_fun might identity needed predictions already desired scale. se.fit Logical; TRUE, includes .se.fit column (currently NA standard errors implemented beezdemand_nlme predictions). interval One "none" (default) "confidence". requested, .lower/.upper returned NA. interval_level Confidence level interval = "confidence". Currently used validation. ... Additional arguments passed nlme::predict.nlme().

title: Predict Method for beezdemand_nlme Objects — predict.beezdemand_nlme
text: tibble containing original newdata columns plus .fitted. requested, .se.fit .lower/.upper included (currently NA).

title: Predict Method for beezdemand_nlme Objects — predict.beezdemand_nlme
code: # \donttest{ data(ko) fit <- fit_demand_mixed(ko, y_var = "y_ll4", x_var = "x",                         id_var = "monkey", equation_form = "zben") #> Generating starting values using method: 'heuristic' #> Using heuristic method for starting values. #> --- Fitting NLME Model --- #> Equation Form: zben #> Param Space: log10 #> NLME Formula: y_ll4 ~ Q0 * exp(-(10^alpha/Q0) * (10^Q0) * x) #> Start values (first few): Q0_int=2.27, alpha_int=-3 #> Number of fixed parameters: 2 (Q0: 1, alpha: 1) # Population-level predictions preds <- predict(fit, level = 0)  # Subject-level predictions preds_subj <- predict(fit, level = 1) # }

what: Create a Pseudo-Log LL4 Transformation Object for ggplot2 — pseudo_ll4_trans
title: Create a Pseudo-Log LL4 Transformation Object for ggplot2 — pseudo_ll4_trans
text: Generates scales::trans object using ll4 transformation. transformation object can passed trans argument ggplot2::scale_x_continuous ggplot2::scale_y_continuous. designed non-negative data handles zero values gracefully. "pseudo" aspect conceptual, similar pseudo_log_trans handles range including zero, transformation ll4.

title: Create a Pseudo-Log LL4 Transformation Object for ggplot2 — pseudo_ll4_trans
code: pseudo_ll4_trans(lambda = 4)

title: Create a Pseudo-Log LL4 Transformation Object for ggplot2 — pseudo_ll4_trans
text: lambda positive numeric scalar, lambda parameter ll4 transformation. Default 4.

title: Create a Pseudo-Log LL4 Transformation Object for ggplot2 — pseudo_ll4_trans
text: trans object (scales package).

title: Create a Pseudo-Log LL4 Transformation Object for ggplot2 — pseudo_ll4_trans
code: # \donttest{ if (require(ggplot2) && require(scales)) {   set.seed(123)   df <- data.frame(     x_vals = c(0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000, NA), # Include 0 and NA     y_vals = c(0, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 2000, 50)   )    # Using pseudo_ll4_trans for the y-axis   ggplot(df, aes(x = x_vals, y = y_vals)) +     geom_point() +     scale_y_continuous(trans = pseudo_ll4_trans(lambda = 4),                        name = "Y-Values (Pseudo-LL4 Scale)") +     ggtitle("Y-Axis with Pseudo-LL4 Transformation")    # Using pseudo_ll4_trans for the x-axis   ggplot(df, aes(x = x_vals, y = y_vals)) +     geom_point() +     scale_x_continuous(trans = pseudo_ll4_trans(lambda = 2), # Different lambda                        name = "X-Values (Pseudo-LL4 Scale)") +     ggtitle("X-Axis with Pseudo-LL4 Transformation") } #> Loading required package: scales #> Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).  # }

title: Run Monte Carlo Simulation Study for Hurdle Demand Model — run_hurdle_monte_carlo
text: Runs Monte Carlo simulation study assess model performance, including bias, standard error estimates, confidence interval coverage.

title: Run Monte Carlo Simulation Study for Hurdle Demand Model — run_hurdle_monte_carlo
code: run_hurdle_monte_carlo(   n_sim = 100,   n_subjects = 100,   true_params = NULL,   n_random_effects = 2,   prices = seq(0, 11, by = 0.5),   stop_at_zero = TRUE,   verbose = TRUE,   seed = NULL )

title: Run Monte Carlo Simulation Study for Hurdle Demand Model — run_hurdle_monte_carlo
text: n_sim Number simulated datasets. Default 100. n_subjects Number subjects per dataset. Default 100. true_params Named list true parameter values. NULL, defaults used simulate_hurdle_data. n_random_effects Number random effects (2 3). Default 2. prices Numeric vector prices. Default seq(0, 11, = 0.5). stop_at_zero Logical; TRUE simulation, subjects stop first zero. Default TRUE. verbose Logical; print progress. Default TRUE. seed Random seed reproducibility.

title: Run Monte Carlo Simulation Study for Hurdle Demand Model — run_hurdle_monte_carlo
text: list : estimates Data frame parameter estimates simulation true_params True parameter values used summary Summary statistics including bias, SE ratio, coverage n_converged Number simulations converged n_sim Total number simulations attempted

title: Run Monte Carlo Simulation Study for Hurdle Demand Model — run_hurdle_monte_carlo
code: # \donttest{ # Run small simulation study (for demonstration) mc_results <- run_hurdle_monte_carlo(n_sim = 10, n_subjects = 50, seed = 123) #> Running 10 Monte Carlo simulations... #>   Simulation 10/10 #> Done. 10/10 simulations converged (100.0%).  # View summary print(mc_results$summary) #>             parameter true_value mean_estimate          bias relative_bias_pct #> beta0           beta0 -2.0000000    -1.9943656  0.0056343897        0.28171948 #> beta1           beta1  1.0000000     0.9563567 -0.0436432966       -4.36432966 #> log_q0         log_q0  2.3025851     2.3060416  0.0034565568        0.15011636 #> k                   k  2.0000000            NA            NA                NA #> alpha           alpha  0.5000000            NA            NA                NA #> logsigma_a logsigma_a  0.0000000    -0.8292708 -0.8292707526                NA #> logsigma_b logsigma_b -0.6931472    -0.7515787 -0.0584315206       -8.42988650 #> logsigma_e logsigma_e -1.2039728    -1.2031988  0.0007740471        0.06429108 #> rho_ab_raw rho_ab_raw  0.3095196     3.3914059  3.0818862996      995.69987094 #>            empirical_se      mean_se    se_ratio coverage_95 n_valid #> beta0        0.17283314 2.928410e-01   1.6943567           1      10 #> beta1        0.32768830 4.440363e-01   1.3550569           1      10 #> log_q0       0.05897284 7.716464e-02   1.3084777           1      10 #> k                    NA           NA          NA          NA       0 #> alpha                NA           NA          NA          NA       0 #> logsigma_a   1.02444987 1.095008e+00   1.0688738           1      10 #> logsigma_b   0.07955811 1.110739e-01   1.3961355           1      10 #> logsigma_e   0.05163320 4.697075e-02   0.9097006           1      10 #> rho_ab_raw   4.07800960 1.152679e+03 282.6573410           1      10  # Check convergence rate cat("Convergence rate:", mc_results$n_converged / mc_results$n_sim, "\n") #> Convergence rate: 1  # }

code: # \donttest{ if (require(ggplot2) && require(scales)) {   set.seed(123)   df <- data.frame(     x = 1:100,     y_raw = c(0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, sample(1:2000, 90, replace = TRUE))   )    # Plot with y-axis on LL4 scale   ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y_raw)) +     geom_point() +     scale_ll4(name = "Y-axis (LL4 Scale)", lambda = 4) +     ggtitle("Data with LL4 Transformed Y-Axis")    # Can also be used for x-axis by replacing scale_y_continuous in its definition   # Or by creating a scale_x_ll4 variant. }  # }

what: Simulate Data from Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — simulate_hurdle_data
title: Simulate Data from Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — simulate_hurdle_data
text: Generates simulated demand data two-part hurdle model. Useful Monte Carlo simulation studies, power analyses, model validation.

title: Simulate Data from Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — simulate_hurdle_data
code: simulate_hurdle_data(   n_subjects = 100,   prices = seq(0, 11, by = 0.5),   beta0 = -2,   beta1 = 1,   log_q0 = log(10),   logQ0 = deprecated(),   k = 2,   alpha = 0.5,   sigma_a = 1,   sigma_b = 0.5,   sigma_c = 0.1,   rho_ab = 0.3,   rho_ac = 0,   rho_bc = 0,   sigma_e = 0.3,   epsilon = 0.001,   n_random_effects = 2,   stop_at_zero = TRUE,   seed = NULL )

title: Simulate Data from Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — simulate_hurdle_data
text: n_subjects Number subjects simulate. Default 100. prices Numeric vector prices simulate consumption. Default seq(0, 11, = 0.5). beta0 Intercept Part (logistic). Default -2. beta1 Slope Part (log(price + epsilon)). Default 1. log_q0 Log intensity parameter (Q0). Default log(10), meaning Q0 = 10. specify Q0 directly, use log_q0 = log(your_Q0). logQ0 Use log_q0 instead. k Scaling parameter demand decay. Default 2. alpha Elasticity parameter controlling rate demand decay. Default 0.5. sigma_a Standard deviation random intercept Part . Default 1. sigma_b Standard deviation random intercept Part II. Default 0.5. sigma_c Standard deviation random slope alpha (used n_random_effects = 3). Default 0.1. rho_ab Correlation a_i b_i. Default 0.3. rho_ac Correlation a_i c_i. Default 0. rho_bc Correlation b_i c_i. Default 0. sigma_e Residual standard deviation. Default 0.3. epsilon Small constant log(price + epsilon). Default 0.001. n_random_effects Number random effects (2 3). Default 2. stop_at_zero Logical; TRUE, stop generating observations subject zero consumption observed. means subjects varying numbers observations. Set FALSE generate prices subjects. Default TRUE. seed Optional random seed reproducibility.

title: Simulate Data from Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — simulate_hurdle_data
text: data frame columns: id Subject identifier x Price value y Simulated consumption (may include zeros) delta Indicator zero consumption (1 = zero, 0 = positive) a_i Subject-specific random effect Part b_i Subject-specific random effect Part II c_i Subject-specific random effect alpha (n_random_effects = 3)

title: Simulate Data from Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — simulate_hurdle_data
text: simulation follows Zhao et al. (2016): Part (Zero vs Positive): $$logit(P(Y=0)) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \log(price + \epsilon) + a_i$$ Part II (Positive Consumption): $$\log(Y | Y > 0) = (\log Q_0 + b_i) + k \cdot (\exp(-(\alpha + c_i) \cdot price) - 1) + \epsilon$$ Random effects \((a_i, b_i)\) \((a_i, b_i, c_i)\) drawn multivariate normal distribution specified variances correlations.

title: Simulate Data from Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Model — simulate_hurdle_data
code: # Simulate with default parameters (2 RE model) sim_data <- simulate_hurdle_data(n_subjects = 100, seed = 123) head(sim_data) #>   id   x        y delta        a_i        b_i #> 1  1 0.0 4.718989     0 -0.5604756 -0.4229137 #> 2  1 0.5 3.887357     0 -0.5604756 -0.4229137 #> 3  1 1.0 1.966397     0 -0.5604756 -0.4229137 #> 4  1 1.5 1.976901     0 -0.5604756 -0.4229137 #> 5  1 2.0 1.847601     0 -0.5604756 -0.4229137 #> 6  1 2.5 2.580423     0 -0.5604756 -0.4229137  # Simulate with custom prices apt_prices <- c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10) sim_apt <- simulate_hurdle_data(n_subjects = 100, prices = apt_prices, seed = 123)  # Simulate with custom parameters (Q0 = 15, alpha = 0.1) sim_custom <- simulate_hurdle_data(   n_subjects = 100,   log_q0 = log(15),   alpha = 0.1,   seed = 123 )  # Simulate 3 RE model sim_3re <- simulate_hurdle_data(   n_subjects = 100,   n_random_effects = 3,   sigma_c = 0.1,   seed = 456 )

code: validate_cp_data(   data,   x_var = "x",   y_var = "y",   id_var = "id",   group_var = "group",   target_var = "target",   required_cols = c("x", "y"),   filter_target = TRUE,   target_level = "alt",   require_id = FALSE )

title: Validate and Filter Cross-Price Demand Data — validate_cp_data
text: data data frame containing cross-price demand data. x_var Character string; name price column. Default "x". non-default, column renamed "x" internally. y_var Character string; name consumption column. Default "y". non-default, column renamed "y" internally. id_var Character string; name subject identifier column. Default "id". non-default, column renamed "id" internally. group_var Character string; name group column. Default "group". non-default, column renamed "group" internally. target_var Character string; name target indicator column. Default "target". non-default, column renamed "target" internally. required_cols Character vector canonical column names check renaming. Default c("x", "y"). filter_target Logical; TRUE data contains "target" column, filters keep rows target == target_level. Default TRUE. target_level Character string; value target column retain filter_target = TRUE. Default "alt". require_id Logical; TRUE, validates "id" column exists data (renaming). Default FALSE.

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: FitCurves() now superseded fit_demand_fixed(). FitCurves() continue work emits soft deprecation warning. new function provides modern S3 interface summary(), tidy(), glance(), predict(), plot() methods. See vignette("migration-guide") migration instructions. FitMeanCurves() now superseded fit_demand_fixed(agg = "Mean") fit_demand_fixed(agg = "Pooled").

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: fit_demand_mixed() now supports Koffarnus et al. (2015) exponentiated equation via equation_form = "koff". enables fitting demand curves using equation form available FitCurves() within modern hierarchical mixed-effects framework. k parameter can user-specified auto-calculated data range.

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: augment.beezdemand_fixed(): Returns .fitted, .resid augment.beezdemand_hurdle(): Returns .fitted, .fitted_link, .fitted_prob, .resid, .resid_response augment.beezdemand_nlme(): Returns .fitted, .resid, .fixed

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: New compare_models() function unified model comparison across beezdemand model classes. Reports AIC, BIC, delta_AIC, delta_BIC, performs likelihood ratio tests models backend nested. New anova() S3 methods comparing nested models: anova.beezdemand_hurdle(): LRT nested hurdle models anova.beezdemand_nlme(): Delegates nlme::anova.lme()

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: New check_demand_model() generic methods model classes. Performs comprehensive diagnostics including convergence checks, boundary condition detection, random effect variance assessment, residual outlier detection. Returns structured diagnostics object issues recommendations. (Named check_demand_model() avoid conflict performance::check_model().) New plot_residuals() function creates diagnostic plots: residuals vs fitted, histogram residuals, Q-Q plots. Works model classes via augment() infrastructure. New plot_qq() function creates Q-Q plots random effects assess normality assumptions hurdle NLME models.

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: model classes now compute alpha_star (normalized alpha, Strategy B; Rzeszutek et al., 2025), makes elasticity parameter comparable across different values k. Available FitCurves() output (columns alpha_star alpha_star_se), tidy() beezdemand_fixed objects, tidy() beezdemand_hurdle objects. Standard errors obtained via delta method. See ?param-registry details.

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: New get_empirical_measures() modern replacement GetEmpirical(). Returns beezdemand_empirical S3 object; access results via $measures. New get_descriptive_summary() modern replacement GetDescriptives(). Returns beezdemand_descriptive S3 object; access results via $statistics. New get_k() modern replacement GetK(). Returns single numeric k value optional verbose output.

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: New confint() methods extracting confidence intervals model classes: beezdemand_fixed, beezdemand_hurdle, beezdemand_nlme, cp_model_nls. New migration guide vignette (vignette("migration-guide")) documenting transition FitCurves() fit_demand_fixed().

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: summary() methods beezdemand_hurdle beezdemand_nlme now return structured summary objects instead printing directly. Use print(summary(fit)) console output. Programmatic access now possible: s <- summary(fit); s$coefficients. fit_demand_hurdle() now fits demand parameters natural-log space (log_q0, log_alpha, log_k) reports back-transformed values; param_space argument removed. fit_cp_nls() now uses log10-parameterized optimizer coefficients (log10_qalone, , log10_beta) across equation forms; "exponential" form fits log10(y) response scale filters y <= 0 warning. predict.cp_model_nls() now always returns y_pred natural y scale; "exponential" additionally returns y_pred_log10 (longer returns y_pred_natural).

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: New fit_demand_fixed() function provides modern interface individual demand curve fitting. Returns structured S3 object summary(), tidy(), glance() methods. wrapper offers functionality FitCurves() standardized API. New systematicity wrappers unified output vocabulary: check_systematic_demand() purchase task data (wraps CheckUnsystematic()) check_systematic_cp() cross-price data (wraps check_unsystematic_cp()) return beezdemand_systematicity objects identical column schemas (differing NA values domain-specific fields). First-class tidy() glance() support now guaranteed across beezdemand model classes. methods return tibbles standardized columns including model_class backend. summary objects now inherit beezdemand_summary base class, enabling shared fallback behavior consistent field availability.

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: release introduces Stability Contracts model classes: summary() objects now return structured S3 objects class c("summary.<class>", "beezdemand_summary"). Required fields include: call, model_class, backend, nobs, n_subjects, converged, logLik, AIC, BIC, coefficients (tibble), notes. tidy() methods return tibbles columns: term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value. Multi-part models include component column (e.g., “fixed”, “variance”, “derived”). glance() methods return 1-row tibbles columns: model_class, backend, nobs, n_subjects, converged, logLik, AIC, BIC.

title: beezdemand 0.2.0
text: fit_cp_nls() fit_cp_linear() now accept x_var/y_var map non-standard column names canonical ones ("x", "y"). fit_cp_linear() additionally accepts id_var, group_var, target_var. Default behavior unchanged arguments omitted. fit_cp_linear() gains explicit filter_target target_level top-level arguments (previously handled implicitly via validate_cp_data()). Existing calls without arguments unaffected. fit_cp_nls(start_vals=) deprecated favor start_values=. old argument still works emits deprecation warning.

title: beezdemand 0.1.3
text: following deprecations take effect version 0.2.0: beezdemand::pull() deprecated favor dplyr::pull(). beezdemand version legacy helper predates dplyr function. inverse_fun argument summary.cp_model_nls(), plot.cp_model_nls(), predict.cp_model_nls() deprecated favor inv_fun consistency mixed-effects model methods.

title: beezdemand 0.1.3
text: Standardized argument names across cross-price model methods (inv_fun instead inverse_fun) Cross-price plot methods now consistent argument ordering across plot.cp_model_nls(), plot.cp_model_lm(), plot.cp_model_lmer() Key user-facing functions now return tibbles better compatibility tidyverse workflows: predict.cp_model_nls(), predict.cp_model_lm(), tidy.cp_model_nls(), glance.cp_model_nls() Added standardized error helpers (validation_error(), fitting_error(), missing_package_error()) consistent error messaging check_unsystematic_cp() now returns object class cp_unsystematic proper summary() method dispatch (longer overrides summary.tbl_df())

what: Two-Part Mixed Effects Hurdle Demand Models
title: beezdemand 0.1.3
text: Added comprehensive hurdle model functionality using TMB (Template Model Builder): fit_demand_hurdle(): Fit two-part hurdle models 2 3 random effects Part models probability zero consumption (logistic regression random intercept) Part II models log-consumption given positive response (nonlinear mixed effects) S3 methods beezdemand_hurdle objects: print(), summary(), coef(), logLik(), AIC(), BIC() predict(): Extract subject parameters predict demand/probability plot(): Visualize demand curves, zero probability, parameter distributions Utility functions: calc_omax_pmax(): Calculate Pmax Omax demand parameters get_subject_pars(): Extract subject-specific parameter estimates compare_hurdle_models(): Likelihood ratio test model comparison get_hurdle_param_summary(): Summary statistics individual parameters Simulation functions: simulate_hurdle_data(): Generate synthetic hurdle model data run_hurdle_monte_carlo(): Monte Carlo simulation studies New vignette “Hurdle Demand Models” comprehensive examples New dataset apt_full: Full alcohol purchase task data 1,100 subjects demographic covariates

title: beezdemand 0.1.3
text: Added comprehensive cross-price demand model functionality: check_unsystematic_cp(): Check unsystematic data cross-price models fit_cp_nls(): Nonlinear model fitting cross-price demand data fit_cp_linear(): Linear model fitting cross-price demand data options fixed effects mixed-effects models New utility functions cross-price model objects: summary(), plot(), glance(), tidy() methods extract_coefficients(): Extract model coefficients tidy format cp_posthoc_slopes() cp_posthoc_intercepts(): Post-hoc comparisons model parameters validate_cp_data(): Validate filter cross-price demand data Added new vignette “Use Cross-Price Demand Model Functions” demonstrating: Required data structure cross-price analyses Checking unsystematic data two-stage pooled model fitting approaches Linear mixed-effects modeling options Model visualization coefficient extraction Post-hoc comparisons

title: beezdemand 0.1.2
text: CRAN release: 2023-08-26 longer relies nlmrt instead relies nlsr Fixes issue CheckUnsystematic may flag certain cases data passed tibble Fixes deprecated arguments ggplot2 Add ability specify start value alpha ExtraF() function

title: beezdemand 0.1.1
text: Add experimental features FitCurves(). arguments constrainq0, startq0, startalpha. arguments allow Q0 constrained alpha fitted parameter allow user-specified starting values.

text: Old code previous workflow removed. Now functions use longform data.
